meta.question: Do eggs increase cardiovascular disease risk?
meta.note: REAL, sourced evidence base (compiled June 2026). Every source carries a citable url, its actual funding disclosure, and the underlying dataset(s) it rests on. `evidence` and `population` use a small controlled vocabulary (so blindspots compute meaningfully); exact sample sizes and study identity live in the title, provenance, and url. Positions and factor weights are the curators' faithful summary of each camp. Verify any entry via its url. See README for how this maps to `discover` + `ingest`.

datasets.label: Lifetime Risk Pooling Project (6 US cohorts)

factors.label: Dietary cholesterol raises serum LDL
factors.rationale: Controlled-feeding trials and the AHA advisory hold that dietary cholesterol raises LDL; the 'no association' cohorts treat this biomarker effect as not translating into hard events.

factors.label: Judge eggs on biomarkers (LDL) vs hard CVD events
factors.rationale: The 'increases risk' camp weights LDL and mortality endpoints (Zhong, Zhuang, Barnard); the 'no association' cohorts find null hard-event associations and weight those.

factors.label: Discount cohorts for healthy-user / residual confounding
factors.rationale: The RCT/feeding camp distrusts observational cohorts (egg-eaters differ in lifestyle); both cohort camps discount little.

factors.label: Subgroup effects (diabetics, hyper-responders)
factors.rationale: Hu 1999 flagged elevated risk in diabetics; DIABEGG tested diabetics directly; all camps now concede subgroups differ — a rare convergence hinting the headline question ('are eggs healthy?') is mis-posed.

factors.label: Weight given to industry funding / conflicts of interest
factors.rationale: Barnard's review weights sponsorship bias heavily (49% of industry-funded studies reported conclusions discordant with their own results, vs 13% of independent ones); industry-backed studies downweight it.

factors.label: Dietary-pattern context (what eggs replace)
factors.rationale: The AHA advisory and substitution analyses stress that eggs' effect depends on what they replace (egg whites, refined carbs, processed meat) and the overall diet.

sources.title: Zhong et al. — Dietary cholesterol/egg & incident CVD and mortality (6 pooled US cohorts, n=29,615)

position.quote: higher consumption of dietary cholesterol or eggs was significantly associated with higher risk of incident CVD and all-cause mortality

sources.title: Barnard et al. — Industry Funding and Cholesterol Research: A Systematic Review (153 feeding studies, 1950–2019)

position.quote: 49% of industry-funded intervention studies reported conclusions that were discordant with study results (net cholesterol increases described as favorable), vs 13% of non-industry-funded studies

sources.title: Hu et al. — Prospective study of egg consumption and CVD in men and women (NHS + HPFS, n~118,000)

position.quote: consumption of up to 1 egg per day is unlikely to have substantial overall impact on the risk of CHD or stroke among healthy men and women

subgroup.quote: increased risk of CHD with higher egg consumption among diabetic subjects

sources.title: Drouin-Chartier et al. — Egg consumption & CVD: three US cohorts + updated meta-analysis (n=215,618; meta 1.72M)

position.quote: moderate egg consumption (up to one egg per day) was not associated with cardiovascular disease risk overall

restsOn.quote: Nurses' Health Study (1980-2012), NHS II (1991-2013), and Health Professionals' Follow-Up Study (1986-2012)

sources.title: Dehghan et al. — Egg intake, lipids, CVD & mortality in 50 countries (PURE, n~177,000)

position.quote: no significant associations were found between egg intake and blood lipids, mortality, or major CVD events

sources.title: Qin et al. — Egg consumption & CVD in 0.5M Chinese adults (China Kadoorie Biobank, n=416,213)

position.quote: daily egg consumption was associated with a lower risk of cardiovascular disease

sources.title: Fuller et al. — High-egg diet in type 2 diabetes (DIABEGG, 12-month RCT, n=128)

position.quote: a high-egg diet did not have an adverse effect on the lipid profile of people with type 2 diabetes

funding.quote: supported by a research grant from the Australian Egg Corporation

sources.title: Blesso/Fernandez et al. — Whole egg vs egg substitute & lipoprotein profiles in metabolic syndrome (RCT, n=37)

position.quote: whole egg consumption improves lipoprotein profiles and insulin sensitivity to a greater extent than yolk-free egg substitute

funding.quote: supported by the Egg Nutrition Center

sources.title: Carson et al. — Dietary Cholesterol and CVD Risk: AHA Science Advisory

position.quote: dietary guidance should focus on healthy dietary patterns... most meta-analyses of intervention studies associate cholesterol intakes exceeding current levels with elevated LDL
